elements.sent-2411-001: We present a study of shearfree gravitational collapse using cylindrically symmetric spacetimes whose interior is a non-rotating dissipative fluid bounded by a cylindrical hypersurface beyond which is an Einstein-Rosen vacuum exterior.
elements.sent-2411-002: We consider three different pressure configurations: axially, azimuthally, and radially directed, for which we find new exact interior solutions of the field equations.
elements.sent-2411-003: We show that the matching conditions cannot be satisfied by the fluid with radial pressure, while the axial and azimuthal cases with a lapse function depending only on the time coordinate do satisfy these constraints.
elements.sent-2411-004: We derive, for both cases, a sufficient condition for an emission of gravitational radiation from the interior towards the exterior, We therefore show that, at variance with what happens for spherical symmetry, in the simplified picture of an infinite cylinder of anisotropic shearfree matter, gravitational waves can be emitted during collapsing motion.
elements.sent-2411-005: In spherical symmetry, gravitational collapse occurs without emission of gravitational waves, owing to Birkhoff's theorem.
elements.sent-2411-006: The next simplest symmetry assumption of cylindrical symmetry has been therefore considered in the literature, mostly for dust, see, e.. g., \cite{G19 .}
elements.sent-2411-007: For an overview of the importance of cylindrical systems and cylindrical gravitational waves in General Relativity (GR), see \cite{B20,S24 .}
elements.sent-2411-008: We establish, here, the conditions allowing a collapsing cylinder of fluid with anisotropic pressure to emit such waves.
elements.sent-2411-009: The study of shearfree cylindrical gravitational collapse, initiated by Di Prisco et al. \cite{DP09 , is thus completed for cylinders of fluids exhibiting particular kinds of anisotropic pressure.}
elements.sent-2411-010: The shearfree approximation is valid in the limit of very slow collapse.
elements.sent-2411-011: A set of three new exact solutions to the field equations of GR, each corresponding to a principal direction of the pressure of the fluid is displayed.
elements.sent-2411-012: However, the matching conditions to an exterior Einstein-Rosen (ER) vacuum can only be satisfied for two of them: the spacetimes sourced by a fluid with axial pressure and those sourced by a fluid with azimuthal pressure.
elements.sent-2411-013: The translational Killing field $\partial_z$, present in a cylindrical spacetime, as well as the rotational Killing field $\partial_{\phi}$, implies that such spacetimes are not generally asymptotically flat.
elements.sent-2411-014: Therefore quantities which are usually determined asymptotically, such as mass or energy, are devoid of meaning here.
elements.sent-2411-015: Thus, the definition of radiation for isolated bodies proposed in \cite{B62 cannot be used.}
elements.sent-2411-016: The conditions to be verified by the parameters defining these solutions to allow the fluid to emit gravitational waves are here obtained from imposing the energy density of the gravitational fluid to decrease with time, while the cylinder is collapsing.
elements.sent-2411-017: We thus identify and expressly quantify the conditions for gravitational waves to occur in a case with only one degree of symmetry less than in the spherical symmetric configuration.
elements.sent-2411-018: The paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-019: The metric and general shearfree conditions are displayed in Sec.\ref{shear .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Then, each pressure configuration is examined in turn: the axial case in Sec.\ref{axial , the azimuthal case in Sec.\ref{azim , and the radial case in Sec.\ref{rad .}}}
elements.sent-2411-021: Only the first two are shown to exhibit gravitational radiation under some conditions which are derived there.
elements.sent-2411-022: Section \ref{concl is devoted to the conclusions.}
elements.sent-2411-023: The source of the considered spacetimes is a cylindrically symmetric anisotropic fluid in collapsing motion.
elements.sent-2411-024: It is non rotating, dissipative and bounded by a cylindrical hypersurface $\Sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-025: In the following, its principal stresses $P_r$, $P_z$ and $P_\phi$ will satisfy three different equations of state which will be analyzed in turn.
elements.sent-2411-026: In geometric units $c=G=1$, the time dependent diagonal line element reads \begin{equation}ds^2=-A^2 (dt^2 - dr^2) + B^2 dz^2 + C^2 d\phi^2, \label{metric}\end{equation} $A$, $B$, and $C$, being real functions of the time coordinate $t$ and of the radial coordinate $r$ such as to account for collapse, which can be viewed as an expansion with a reversal of the time coordinate.
elements.sent-2411-027: The cylindrical symmetry forces the coordinates to conform to the following ranges: \begin{equation}0 \leq t \leq +\infty, \quad 0 \leq r \leq +\infty, \quad -\infty \leq z \leq +\infty, \quad 0 \leq \phi \leq 2 \pi, \label{ranges}\end{equation} where $t = 0$ represents the beginning of the collapse, and where the two limits of the coordinate $\phi$ are topologically identified.
elements.sent-2411-029: Here, we assume that the collapse is sufficiently slow so as to justify a vanishing shear approximation.
elements.sent-2411-030: The non-zero components of the shear tensor corresponding to the diagonal metric (\ref{metric ) are \cite{DP09 \begin{equation}\sigma_{11} = \frac{A}{3} \left(\frac{2\dot{A}}{A} - \frac{\dot{B}}{B} - \frac{\dot{C}}{C}\right), \label{shear1}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\sigma_{22} = \frac{B^2}{3A} \left(\frac{2\dot{B}}{B} - \frac{\dot{A}}{A} - \frac{\dot{C}}{C}\right), \label{shear2}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\sigma_{33} = \frac{C^2}{3A} \left(\frac{2\dot{C}}{C} - \frac{\dot{A}}{A} - \frac{\dot{B}}{B}\right). \label{shear3}\end{equation} Assuming that the motion of the collapsing fluid is shearfree implies \begin{equation}\frac{\dot{A}}{A} = \frac{\dot{B}}{B} = \frac{\dot{C}}{C}, \label{shear4}\end{equation} which can be integrated by \begin{equation}B = \beta(r) A, \label{shear5}\end{equation} \begin{equation}C = \chi(r) A. \label{shear6}\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-031: In this section, $P_r= P_{\phi =0$, which means that the pressure of the gravitational fluid is axially directed.}
elements.sent-2411-032: Its stress-energy tensor, whose general expression has been given by (1) of C\'el\'erier and Santos \cite{CS20 , can therefore be written under the form \begin{equation}T_{\alpha \beta} = \rho V_{\alpha}V_{\beta} + P_z S_\alpha S_\beta, \label{1ax}\end{equation} where $\rho$ denotes the energy density of the fluid, $V_\alpha$, its timelike 4-velocity, and $S_\alpha$, a spacelike 4-vector satisfying \begin{equation}V^\alpha V_\alpha = -1, \quad S^\alpha S_\alpha = 1, \quad V^\alpha S_\alpha =0. \label{2ax}\end{equation} Collapse allows the choice of a frame comoving with the fluid \cite{DP09 .}}

elements.sent-2411-140: The cylindrical collapse of shearfree anisotropic fluids matched to an exterior ER vacuum has been analyzed here through the use of new exact analytic interior solutions displayed for the first time.
elements.sent-2411-141: This work is a follow up of a previous study by Di Prisco et al. \cite{DP09 where the Robertson-Walker dust solution had been considered, but had failed to exhibit any radiation transfer to or from the interior.}
elements.sent-2411-142: Here, we have taken anisotropy into account by dividing the problem into three different configurations, each with the pressure along one of the principal directions, i.
elements.sent-2411-143: The three classes of exact solutions found for each pressure configuration have been matched to the ER vacuum exterior.
elements.sent-2411-144: Thus, we have shown that the Darmois matching conditions cannot be satisfied by the fluid with radial pressure, while the axial and azimuthal cases with a lapse function depending only on the time coordinate do satisfy this constraint.
elements.sent-2411-145: Then, we have established for both these axial and azimuthal subcases a sufficient condition for an emission of radiation from the interior towards the exterior, This shows that, while an isolated spherically symmetric collapsing object cannot emit gravitational waves, cylindrical ones with axial or azimuthal pressure can, under the conditions displayed in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-146: Indeed, in the realm of GR, spherical gravitational waves are forbidden by Birkhoff's theorem, while this theorem does not apply to the case of cylindrical symmetry.
elements.sent-2411-147: Of course, the shearfree assumption retained here is only valid for a slowly collapsing object.
elements.sent-2411-148: It might break down at the end of the collapse when the pressure fails to counteract sufficiently gravitation.
elements.sent-2411-149: However, should this simplifying assumption be given up, analytic solutions such as the ones used here could no more arise.
elements.sent-2411-150: Anyhow, the results displayed here can increase our understanding of gravitational radiation, by showing that, even in the simplified picture of an infinite cylinder of anisotropic shearfree matter, gravitational waves can be emitted during collapsing motion.
elements.sent-2411-151: We derive, for both cases, a sufficient condition for an emission of gravitational radiation from the interior towards the exterior.
elements.sent-2411-152: Therefore we show that, at variance with what happens for spherical symmetry, in the simplified picture of an infinite cylinder of anisotropic shearfree matter, gravitational waves can be emitted during collapsing motion.
elements.sent-2411-153: The next simplest symmetry assumption of cylindrical symmetry has been, therefore, considered in the literature, mostly for dust, see, e.. g., \cite{G19 .}
elements.sent-2411-154: Now, cylindrical systems may seem physically irrelevant since they impose infinitely long sources.
elements.sent-2411-155: However, under controlled circumstances, they can yield interesting physical descriptions that otherwise might be extremely difficult to tackle mathematically.
elements.sent-2411-156: For instance, this is the case with exact models of spacetime rotation or dragging, models of extragalactic jets, gravitational radiation, translating fluids which might represent light beams produced by stars, and many others.
elements.sent-2411-157: We establish here the conditions allowing a collapsing cylinder of fluid with anisotropic pressure to emit such waves.
elements.sent-2411-158: A set of three new exact solutions to the field equations of GR, each corresponding to a principal direction of the fluid pressure, is displayed.
elements.sent-2411-159: However, the matching conditions to an exterior Einstein-Rosen vacuum can only be satisfied for two of them: the spacetimes sourced by a fluid with axial pressure and those sourced by a fluid with azimuthal pressure.
elements.sent-2411-160: Therefore, quantities which are usually determined asymptotically, such as mass or energy, are devoid of meaning here.
elements.sent-2411-161: The conditions to be verified by the parameters defining these solutions to allow the fluid to emit gravitational waves are here obtained by imposing the energy density of the gravitational fluid to decrease with time, while the cylinder is collapsing.
elements.sent-2411-162: The source of the spacetimes is a collapsing cylindrically symmetric anisotropic fluid.
elements.sent-2411-163: It is non-rotating, dissipative and bounded by a cylindrical hypersurface $\Sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-164: At some initial time, $t = t_i$, the expansion of the fluid lines is negative and the cylinder starts collapsing.
elements.sent-2411-165: At $t = 0$ the collapse ends in a singularity.
